does: This paper proposes a structured framework for how frontier AI companies could establish internal audit functions to oversee catastrophic and systemic risks. The authors draw on professional internal auditing standards and risk-based assurance theory to analyze four core design dimensions: audit scope (covering model-level, system-level, and governance-level controls), sourcing arrangements (in-house, co-sourced, or outsourced), audit frequency and cadence, and access to sensitive information. For each dimension, the paper maps the relevant option space and assesses the benefits and limitations of different choices. The work provides practical analysis of trade-offs inherent in structuring internal assurance mechanisms within frontier AI organizations, grounding recommendations in established auditing practices while addressing domain-specific challenges.
connects: This work builds on emerging frontier AI governance literature and professional internal auditing standards that have been developed across other high-risk industries. It responds to the observation that while external evaluations and safety frameworks for AI have proliferated, comparatively little systematic attention has been given to internal organizational assurance structures. The paper extends risk-based assurance frameworks—well-established in financial services, healthcare, and other regulated sectors—to the specific context of AI governance. It engages with growing literature on how to structure meaningful oversight of catastrophic risks and complements recent work on external AI evaluations by addressing the internal mechanisms through which companies can provide evidence-based oversight of their own development practices.
enables: This framework enables frontier AI companies to design internal audit functions that can credibly assess and monitor risks during rapid development cycles. By clarifying design dimensions and trade-offs, it facilitates more informed governance decisions about where to allocate oversight resources and how to structure accountability mechanisms internally. The work points toward hybrid approaches combining internal expertise with external scrutiny, and establishes foundations for regulatory engagement by demonstrating how internal assurance could provide sustained, evidence-based oversight. It opens pathways for regulators and stakeholders to understand what meaningful internal governance looks like in practice, potentially informing future regulatory standards. The framework also enables comparative analysis of different organizational governance structures and their relative effectiveness in managing catastrophic risks.
